responses: Conjunctivitis, commonly known as 'pink eye,' is an inflammation or infection of the transparent membrane (conjunctiva) that lines your eyelid and covers the white part of your eyeball.
When small blood vessels in the conjunctiva become inflamed, they're more visible. This is what causes the whites of your eyes to appear reddish or pink.

patterns: Is pink eye viral or bacterial?
responses: The most common causes are viruses and bacteria. Other causes include allergies (to pollen or smoke), a chemical splash in the eye, or a foreign object in the eye.
Viral and bacterial pink eye are highly contagious and spread through direct contact with eye secretions or contaminated surfaces. Allergic pink eye is not contagious.

Symptoms include redness in one or both eyes, itchiness, a gritty feeling (like sand in the eye), and a discharge that forms a crust during the night that may prevent your eye from opening in the morning.

Viral pink eye usually produces a watery discharge, while bacterial pink eye often produces a thicker, yellow-green discharge. Allergic pink eye typically causes intense itching and tearing in both eyes.
Don't touch your eyes with your hands. Wash your hands often, use a clean towel and washcloth daily, and don't share towels or eye cosmetics.
If you have pink eye, stay home from work or school until the discharge has stopped to avoid infecting others. Disinfect surfaces like doorknobs and shared electronics.

patterns: What to eat for pink eye?
responses: While food won't cure pink eye, Vitamin A (found in carrots, sweet potatoes, and leafy greens) is essential for maintaining a healthy conjunctiva and cornea.
Omega-3 fatty acids (found in flaxseeds and fatty fish) can help reduce the inflammation associated with allergic conjunctivitis and dry eye symptoms.

patterns: How to treat pink eye at home?
Do I need antibiotics for red eye?
responses: Viral pink eye usually clears up on its own in 1-2 weeks; artificial tears and cold compresses can help with discomfort. Bacterial pink eye may require antibiotic eye drops or ointments prescribed by a doctor.

Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops or oral allergy medications. WARNING: Do not use 'redness-relief' drops (vasoconstrictors) for more than two days, as they can cause 'rebound' redness.

patterns: What to do after pink eye?
How to clean eyes after infection?
Should I throw away my makeup after pink eye?
When can I wear contacts again?
responses: Throw away any eye makeup (mascara, eyeliner) used while you had the infection to prevent reinfecting yourself. Thoroughly clean your eyeglasses and discard any disposable contact lenses.
Wait at least 24 hours after your symptoms have completely disappeared before wearing contact lenses again. Disinfect your contact lens case or, better yet, replace it with a new one.
